title: Iran and the new Persian Gulf equilibrium
gist: Having failed to deter Israeli and US strikes through its Axis of Resistance proxies, Iran is permanently pivoting its deterrence strategy toward the demonstrated threat of closing the Strait of Hormuz, creating a lasting overhang on Gulf shipping and regional stability even as a ceasefire deal eventually emerges.

insights.claim: The Iran-US ceasefire, in place two months as of mid-June 2026, has been punctuated by violent episodes including the shooting down of a US helicopter and Trump's 11 June threat to seize Iran's Kharg Island, yet both sides have generally avoided returning to open warfare.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Expect continued limited escalations rather than a full resumption of war; monitor Kharg Island and Iranian oil-export infrastructure as flashpoints.
insights.locator: The fragile ceasefire between Iran and the United States

insights.claim: The US signaled to Iran through Qatar that its recent strikes were not intended as a resumption of all-out war, and negotiations to prolong the ceasefire and reopen the Strait of Hormuz have stalled, though a deal will eventually emerge.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A Hormuz reopening deal is likely but not imminent; near-term shipping risk premiums through the Strait should stay elevated.
insights.locator: According to media reports the US has signaled to Iran

insights.claim: Iran and the US each perceive they hold the upper hand and expect the other to compromise, leaving them in a volatile 'no war, no peace' state since the early-April ceasefire, unresolved after 47 years of bilateral divergence.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This stalemate framing implies no durable bilateral settlement is near; limited escalations should be treated as the baseline, not the exception.
insights.locator: Each perceives that it has the upper hand

themes.theme: Failure of the Axis of Resistance

insights.claim: Iran's Axis of Resistance — the network of non-state armed groups including Hamas and Hezbollah — failed to deliver its core original purpose of deterrence: the threat of costly retaliation from Axis members did not stop multiple American and Israeli attacks on the Iranian homeland.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The collapse of proxy-based deterrence is the causal trigger for Iran's strategic pivot toward chokepoint leverage — a structural shift, not a temporary tactic.
insights.locator: the Axis of Resistance – the network of non-state armed groups

insights.claim: What was long known only in theory through wargames and simulations — that Iran would try to close the Strait of Hormuz if its survival was threatened — has now been demonstrated in practice, and 'there will be no turning back.'
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Treat Hormuz closure risk as a now-proven, repeatable tool rather than a theoretical tail risk when pricing Gulf oil shipping and tanker insurance.
insights.quote: The war, in other words, has broken a psychological barrier that will not be rebuilt.
insights.locator: What was known in theory has now been demonstrated in practice

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz will eventually reopen to maritime traffic, but gradually rather than suddenly, both for security reasons and because it will take time for supply chains to reorganize.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Expect a slow, phased normalization of Gulf shipping volumes rather than a sharp snap-back once a deal is reached — plan logistics and freight-rate expectations accordingly.
insights.locator: There is little doubt that the Strait of Hormuz will eventually re-open

insights.claim: Iran will not forgo the option of closing the Strait again and will integrate the tactic permanently into its strategy, ensuring the closure threat remains 'a black cloud permanently hanging over maritime shipping in the Gulf and, therefore, the global economy.'
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A structural, persistent Hormuz risk premium should now be priced into global oil and shipping markets rather than treated as an episodic crisis event.
insights.quote: a black cloud permanently hanging over maritime shipping in the Gulf and, therefore, the global economy
insights.locator: But the Islamic Republic will not forget the tremendous leverage

insights.claim: Iran will prioritize rebuilding its missile and drone production facilities — heavily damaged by American and Israeli strikes — and diversifying its global supply networks ahead of rebuilding its shattered nuclear infrastructure and conventional navy.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Iran's reconstruction priorities signal that missile/drone (asymmetric, Hormuz-relevant) capability rebuilding — not nuclear or naval buildup — is the near-term proliferation and sanctions-enforcement risk to monitor.
insights.quote: Rebuilding its missile and drone production facilities…will be [Iran’s] top priority, as will be the consolidation and diversification of its global supply networks.
insights.locator: Iran will, as such, restock and reconstitute its damaged military infrastructure

insights.claim: The Houthis, who control northwestern Yemen, are not Iranian puppets but caused severe Red Sea shipping disruption in 2023-25 in solidarity with Palestinians and this week explicitly threatened to close the Bab al-Mandab strait to Israeli shipping; in a future conflict where Iran feels seriously threatened, they could renew such attacks.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A simultaneous Hormuz and Bab al-Mandab disruption is a plausible tail-risk combination with outsized impact on global shipping routes and oil/container freight costs.
insights.locator: The fear of that scenario will be compounded

insights.claim: The new Gulf equilibrium also includes a standing threat of Iranian attacks on the six GCC petro-monarchies (Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE) if Iran is pushed into a corner, a precedent the war has now established as real leverage rather than theory.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: GCC states' investment-grade 'haven of stability' branding now carries a durable geopolitical risk discount that wasn't priced in before the 2026 war.
insights.locator: The other feature of the new equilibrium
